From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French daily newspaper founded in 1944

For the book by René Descartes, see [*The World* (Descartes)](/wiki/The_World_(Descartes) "The World (Descartes)"). For other uses, see [Monde (disambiguation)](/wiki/Monde_(disambiguation) "Monde (disambiguation)").

***Le Monde*** (French: [[l(ə) mɔ̃d]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); "The World") is a French newspaper founded in 1944 by [Hubert Beuve-Méry](/wiki/Hubert_Beuve-M%C3%A9ry "Hubert Beuve-Méry"). It is the most widely read paid national daily newspaper in France, with 2.44 million readers in 2021, and the most widely circulated, with around 500,000 subscribers, including 414,000 digital subscribers and 87,000 print subscribers.

It presents itself as a "[newspaper of record](/wiki/Newspaper_of_record "Newspaper of record")".[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Former editor Éric Fottorino preferred not to describe the newspaper as a "newspaper of record", stating instead that it was "not just any newspaper", but rather one that "claims to become the reference, an alloy of competence and editorial independence built over several decades".[[6]](#cite_note-6) It is nevertheless widely regarded as such,[[7]](#cite_note-7) including internationally.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

*Le Monde* is the last French daily traditionally described as an "evening paper". It is published in Paris in the early afternoon with the following day's date, and later in some major cities, before being distributed elsewhere the next morning.

According to [Radio France](/wiki/Radio_France "Radio France"), *Le Monde* has adopted successive editorial lines since its founding, generally located, albeit reductively, on the [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics").[[11]](#cite_note-11) A 2010 academic work also characterises its editorial stance as centre-left.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In April 2022, an [Ifop](/wiki/Institut_fran%C3%A7ais_d%27opinion_publique "Institut français d'opinion publique") survey indicated that among regular readers of *Le Monde*, 48% voted for left-wing candidates in the first round of the [2012](/wiki/2012_French_presidential_election "2012 French presidential election") presidential election, and 27% voted for [Emmanuel Macron](/wiki/Emmanuel_Macron "Emmanuel Macron").

*Le Monde* is owned by [Groupe Le Monde](/wiki/Groupe_Le_Monde "Groupe Le Monde"). Its shareholding structure includes the holding company [Le Monde libre](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde_libre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Le Monde libre (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_Monde_libre_(entreprise) "fr:Le Monde libre (entreprise)")], notably owned by [Xavier Niel](/wiki/Xavier_Niel "Xavier Niel") and [Matthieu Pigasse](/wiki/Matthieu_Pigasse "Matthieu Pigasse"), and the [Independence Pole](/w/index.php?title=Independence_Pole&action=edit&redlink=1 "Independence Pole (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/P%C3%B4le_d%27ind%C3%A9pendance_du_Groupe_Le_Monde "fr:Pôle d'indépendance du Groupe Le Monde")], held by employees, trade unions, and associations.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1944–1968: Foundation and establishment as a newspaper of record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1944–1968: Foundation and establishment as a newspaper of record")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/5%2C_rue_des_italiens%2C_Paris%2C_France.JPG/250px-5%2C_rue_des_italiens%2C_Paris%2C_France.JPG)](/wiki/File:5,_rue_des_italiens,_Paris,_France.JPG)

The editorial offices of *Le Monde* were located at 5, Rue des Italiens, Paris, from its creation until 1989 (photograph taken in 2015).

The first issue of *Le Monde* was published on 18 December 1944,[[14]](#cite_note-14) dated 19 December 1944, and consisted of a single double-sided page. It succeeded the newspaper *[Le Temps](/wiki/Le_Temps_(Paris) "Le Temps (Paris)")*, which had been shut down under the [Ordinances of 1944 on the press](/w/index.php?title=Ordinances_of_1944_on_the_press&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ordinances of 1944 on the press (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordonnances_de_1944_sur_la_presse "fr:Ordonnances de 1944 sur la presse")] targeting publications that appeared during the [German occupation of France during World War II](/wiki/German_occupation_of_France_during_World_War_II "German occupation of France during World War II"). The premises of *Le Temps* were requisitioned and its equipment seized. *Le Monde*, as beneficiary of this confiscation, adopted its format and layout, took over much of its editorial staff, workers, and employees, as well as its former offices on Rue des Italiens, where it would remain for 44 years. This location earned it the nickname "the Rue des Italiens daily".

[General de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle"), who wished to provide [France](/wiki/France "France") with a "prestige newspaper" oriented toward international affairs and serving as an unofficial voice of the Republic, was a driving force behind its creation.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno199653-15) He tasked his [Minister of Information](/wiki/Ministry_of_Information_(France) "Ministry of Information (France)"), [Pierre-Henri Teitgen](/wiki/Pierre-Henri_Teitgen "Pierre-Henri Teitgen"), with finding a director, a difficult task since many press figures of the time had either collaborated during the occupation or were already leading newspapers of the [clandestine press](/wiki/French_Resistance "French Resistance").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno200124-16) [Georges Bidault](/wiki/Georges_Bidault "Georges Bidault"), president of the [National Council of the Resistance](/wiki/National_Council_of_the_Resistance "National Council of the Resistance"), suggested Hubert Beuve-Méry. Beuve-Méry hesitated for a long time, as he sought to run a newspaper independent of political, economic, and religious powers.

On 11 December 1944, Hubert Beuve-Méry founded the limited liability company (SARL) *Le Monde* with capital of 200,000 francs divided into 200 shares. Its first editorial committee also included [René Courtin](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Courtin "René Courtin"), a professor of law, and Christian Funck-Brentano, formerly responsible for press matters in General de Gaulle's cabinet.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno200433-17) Like *Le Temps*, the daily was intended for elites, and reached a circulation of 150,000 copies as early as 1945. Born in the shadow of political power, *Le Monde* gradually emancipated itself under Beuve-Méry, who secured its editorial independence during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") and the [First Indochina War](/wiki/First_Indochina_War "First Indochina War").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Employees have played a central role in the management of the newspaper. In 1951, the *Le Monde* Journalists' Society was created to safeguard the paper's [editorial independence](/wiki/Editorial_independence "Editorial independence"). It was initially allocated just over 28% of the shares of SARL Le Monde.[[19]](#cite_note-19) (This was followed by companies representing employees and managers in 1968, and readers in 1985.) In 1956, *Le Monde* became the owner of its building at 5 Rue des Italiens. From the early 1960s onward, its circulation expanded rapidly, tripling over 20 years, from 137,433 copies in 1960 to 347,783 in 1971, and nearly 500,000 by the late 1970s.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

This financial and editorial independence was also political. The newspaper became a meeting point for several major currents of thought, primarily linked to [Christian democracy](/wiki/Christian_democracy "Christian democracy") in domestic affairs and to moderate [anti-colonialism](/wiki/Anti-colonialism "Anti-colonialism") in foreign policy.

These positions generated debate. In addition to tensions with de Gaulle, [Jean-Jacques Servan-Schreiber](/wiki/Jean-Jacques_Servan-Schreiber "Jean-Jacques Servan-Schreiber"), head of the foreign affairs section, left the newspaper in the early 1950s, criticizing what he saw as its neutralism in East–West relations.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In 1954, *[Le Monde diplomatique](/wiki/Le_Monde_diplomatique "Le Monde diplomatique")* was launched.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In 1955–1956, the [Conseil national du patronat français](/wiki/Conseil_national_du_patronat_fran%C3%A7ais "Conseil national du patronat français"), chaired by [Georges Villiers](/wiki/Georges_Villiers "Georges Villiers"), considered *Le Monde* too left-leaning and decided to support the launch of a competing daily, *[Le Temps de Paris](/w/index.php?title=Le_Temps_de_Paris&action=edit&redlink=1 "Le Temps de Paris (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_Temps_de_Paris "fr:Le Temps de Paris")]*. The operation was coordinated by [Jean Jardin](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Jardin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean Jardin (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_Jardin "fr:Jean Jardin")], a former close adviser to [Pierre Laval](/wiki/Pierre_Laval "Pierre Laval"). When the first issue appeared in March 1956, Beuve-Méry was reassured by what he considered the competitor's mediocre quality; publication ceased after only a few months.

In 1957, the newspaper refused to publish an article by [Jean-Paul Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre") on the use of [torture during the Algerian War](/wiki/Torture_during_the_Algerian_War "Torture during the Algerian War").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Under the [Fifth Republic](/wiki/French_Fifth_Republic "French Fifth Republic"), the newspaper supported General de Gaulle's foreign policy,[[24]](#cite_note-24) while criticizing his domestic policies.

### 1969–1981: Evening newspaper of the centre-left

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1969–1981: Evening newspaper of the centre-left")]

Hubert Beuve-Méry, the founder of the newspaper, retired in 1969.[[25]](#cite_note-25) During the 1970s, the paper clearly moved toward supporting the [Union of the Left](/wiki/Union_of_the_Left_(France) "Union of the Left (France)")[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno200124-16) and denounced the financial scandals that erupted under the Presidency of Valéry Giscard d'Estaing (notably the [Diamonds Affair](/wiki/Diamonds_Affair "Diamonds Affair")). The strong hostility of the newspaper's journalists toward [Valéry Giscard d'Estaing](/wiki/Val%C3%A9ry_Giscard_d%27Estaing "Valéry Giscard d'Estaing") was examined in a 2014 investigation entitled *Le jour où... « Le Monde » choisit de torpiller Giscard* ("The day when... 'Le Monde' chose to torpedo Giscard").[[26]](#cite_note-Bacque-26)

In her investigation, Raphaëlle Bacqué revisited the Diamond affair as it was experienced within *Le Monde* and emphasized the highly political nature of its coverage. She noted in particular the general hostility of the newsroom's journalists toward Giscard d'Estaing and their closeness to the Socialist and Communist opposition. She also described internal debates between those, such as political editor Raymond Barillon, who were cautious and reluctant to relay the revelations of *[Le Canard enchaîné](/wiki/Le_Canard_encha%C3%AEn%C3%A9 "Le Canard enchaîné")*, and others, such as columnist Philippe Boucher, who strongly opposed "Giscardism" and wanted to push the story further by linking it to additional revelations, including those reported by *[Minute](/wiki/Minute_(French_newspaper) "Minute (French newspaper)")* concerning a building permit obtained by [Raymond Barre](/wiki/Raymond_Barre "Raymond Barre") and information about the African assets of Giscard's cousins. Philippe Boucher, later appointed to the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(France) "Council of State (France)") by [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand"), acknowledged in 2014 that he had been somewhat excessive in his handling of the affair.[[26]](#cite_note-Bacque-26)

At the time, although the editorial line did not explicitly claim to be left-wing, it was generally sympathetic to revolutionary socialist movements ([Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), the [Portuguese Revolution](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution")), going so far as to headline "[Phnom Penh](/wiki/Phnom_Penh "Phnom Penh") liberated" when the city was taken by the [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") in April 1975.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 1981, [Claude Julien](/wiki/Claude_Julien_(journalist) "Claude Julien (journalist)") succeeded [Jacques Fauvet](/wiki/Jacques_Fauvet "Jacques Fauvet"). Readership was then at its peak. The newspaper supported the candidacy of François Mitterrand in the [1981 French presidential election](/wiki/1981_French_presidential_election "1981 French presidential election").[[28]](#cite_note-28) After the Socialist candidate's victory, Jacques Fauvet wrote in the 11 May 1981 issue:

> This victory is finally that of respect over disdain, realism over illusion, frankness over artifice—in short, that of a certain morality.

Following the election, the newspaper's open support for François Mitterrand cost it a significant number of readers.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### 1982–1994: Financial and editorial difficulties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1982–1994: Financial and editorial difficulties")]

In 1985, [André Laurens](/w/index.php?title=Andr%C3%A9_Laurens&action=edit&redlink=1 "André Laurens (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Laurens "fr:André Laurens")], who had succeeded Claude Julien in 1982, was removed from the newspaper's leadership following a decline in circulation. While the paper had sold an average of 434,000 copies between 1974 and 1981, circulation fell to 335,000 copies in 1985, dropping below the break-even point.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno200433-17) Laurens was criticized in particular for his relationship with [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand")'s brand of socialism.

He was replaced by [André Fontaine](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Fontaine "André Fontaine").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Under Fontaine, the editorial line distanced itself from Mitterrandism, notably expressing skepticism about the nationalization policy pursued by [Pierre Mauroy](/wiki/Pierre_Mauroy "Pierre Mauroy").[[31]](#cite_note-31) The [sinking of the *Rainbow Warrior*](/wiki/Sinking_of_the_Rainbow_Warrior "Sinking of the Rainbow Warrior") in particular allowed the newspaper to demonstrate its independence and led to a rebound in sales.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno200152-32) *Le Monde* subsequently took a leading role in exposing scandals of the Mitterrand era, such as the [Irish of Vincennes affair](/wiki/Irish_of_Vincennes "Irish of Vincennes") and the [Carrefour du développement affair](/w/index.php?title=Carrefour_du_d%C3%A9veloppement_affair&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carrefour du développement affair (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Affaire_du_Carrefour_du_d%C3%A9veloppement "fr:Affaire du Carrefour du développement")].[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno200148-33) A marked animosity then developed between Mitterrand and the newspaper, targeting in particular journalist [Edwy Plenel](/wiki/Edwy_Plenel "Edwy Plenel").[[34]](#cite_note-34) Several *Le Monde* journalists were subjected to [illegal wiretapping by the authorities](/w/index.php?title=Illegal_wiretapping_by_the_authorities&action=edit&redlink=1 "Illegal wiretapping by the authorities (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Affaire_des_%C3%A9coutes_de_l%27%C3%89lys%C3%A9e "fr:Affaire des écoutes de l'Élysée")].[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In 1985, [BNP](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas") required the newspaper to sell its historic building on the Rue des Italiens.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012151-36) *Le Monde* moved to 15 [Rue Falguière](/w/index.php?title=Rue_Falgui%C3%A8re&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rue Falguière (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rue_Falgui%C3%A8re "fr:Rue Falguière")] ([15th arrondissement](/wiki/15th_arrondissement_of_Paris "15th arrondissement of Paris")) in April 1989, into a building designed by architects Pierre du Besset and [Dominique Lyon](/w/index.php?title=Dominique_Lyon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dominique Lyon (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dominique_Lyon "fr:Dominique Lyon")]. It later relocated to 21 bis [Rue Claude-Bernard](/w/index.php?title=Rue_Claude-Bernard&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rue Claude-Bernard (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rue_Claude-Bernard "fr:Rue Claude-Bernard")] ([5th arrondissement](/wiki/5th_arrondissement_of_Paris "5th arrondissement of Paris")) in 1996, and finally, in 2004, to [Boulevard Auguste-Blanqui](/wiki/Boulevard_Auguste-Blanqui "Boulevard Auguste-Blanqui") ([13th arrondissement](/wiki/13th_arrondissement_of_Paris "13th arrondissement of Paris")) in a building designed by architect [Christian de Portzamparc](/wiki/Christian_de_Portzamparc "Christian de Portzamparc"), whose architecture was inspired by the former headquarters of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In 1989, due to competition from *[Libération](/wiki/Lib%C3%A9ration_(newspaper) "Libération (newspaper)")* and the revival of *[Le Figaro](/wiki/Le_Figaro "Le Figaro")*, circulation had declined by 40,000 copies over a ten-year period.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012159-38)

In February 1990, a [triumvirate](/wiki/Triumvirate "Triumvirate") was appointed to succeed André Fontaine. Composed of [Daniel Vernet](/wiki/Daniel_Vernet "Daniel Vernet") (managing director), [Bruno Frappat](/w/index.php?title=Bruno_Frappat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bruno Frappat (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruno_Frappat "fr:Bruno Frappat")] (editor-in-chief), and Martin Desprez (managing director for administration), it was ultimately replaced, following internal rivalries, by Bruno Frappat, who remained head of the newsroom, and economist [Jacques Lesourne](/wiki/Jacques_Lesourne "Jacques Lesourne"). Lesourne was elected director of publication of *Le Monde* on 8 January 1991, becoming the first non-journalist to hold the position.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno2004468-39)

### 1994–2003: Colombani's expansion strategy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1994–2003: Colombani's expansion strategy")]

In 1994, *Le Monde* changed its legal status from a [limited liability company](/wiki/Limited_liability_company "Limited liability company") (*société à responsabilité limitée*, SARL) to a [public limited company](/wiki/Public_limited_company "Public limited company") (*société anonyme*, SA) with an [executive board](/wiki/Executive_board "Executive board") and a [supervisory board](/wiki/Supervisory_board "Supervisory board"). Following the resignation of Jacques Lesourne, who had been unable to halt the decline in circulation and advertising revenue, [Jean-Marie Colombani](/wiki/Jean-Marie_Colombani "Jean-Marie Colombani"), editor-in-chief, was elected director of publication of the newspaper in March 1994,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012159-38) first by the journalists' association and then by the newspaper's shareholders. In April 1994, he appointed Noël-Jean Bergeroux as editor-in-chief. In 1995, Colombani launched a new format for the daily newspaper.

During the [1995 French presidential election](/wiki/1995_French_presidential_election "1995 French presidential election"), Colombani's hostility toward [Jacques Chirac](/wiki/Jacques_Chirac "Jacques Chirac") following the [Ouvéa cave hostage taking](/wiki/Ouv%C3%A9a_cave_hostage_taking "Ouvéa cave hostage taking"), the anti-Mitterrand stance of Edwy Plenel,[[Note 1]](#cite_note-40) and the globalist outlook associated with [Édouard Balladur](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Balladur "Édouard Balladur") and promoted by [Alain Minc](/wiki/Alain_Minc "Alain Minc"), chairman of the supervisory board of SA Le Monde, led the newspaper to be accused by its peers of "Balladurism."[[40]](#cite_note-41) *[Le Canard enchaîné](/wiki/Le_Canard_encha%C3%AEn%C3%A9 "Le Canard enchaîné")* headlined on 18 January 1995, "Le Monde Balladurized? It's not a Minc affair," a controversy that unsettled part of its readership.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

After an initial [recapitalization](/wiki/Capital_increase "Capital increase") of 295 million francs in 1995, *Le Monde* launched its presence on the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") in 1996. [LeMonde.fr](/wiki/LeMonde.fr "LeMonde.fr") offered online feature packages, a graphical version of the front page from 1:00 p.m., the full newspaper before 5:00 p.m., current news in cooperation with the [Agence France-Presse](/wiki/Agence_France-Presse "Agence France-Presse"), and sections devoted to the stock market, books, multimedia, and sports. Two years later, full online access to the newspaper cost five [French francs](/wiki/French_franc "French franc") (equivalent to €0.76), compared with 7.50 francs (€1.15) for the printed edition.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Some articles from the weekly print supplement *Télévision-Radio-Multimédia* were made freely available online in a multimedia section later renamed "New Technologies."

Re-elected in 2000, Jean-Marie Colombani undertook the construction of a media group, the [Groupe Le Monde](/wiki/Groupe_Le_Monde "Groupe Le Monde"). After an unsuccessful attempt to acquire *[L'Express](/wiki/L%27Express "L'Express")* from Vivendi Universal Publishing (formerly Havas) in 1997,[[43]](#cite_note-44) he took control of the [Les Journaux du Midi](/w/index.php?title=Les_Journaux_du_Midi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Les Journaux du Midi (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Les_Journaux_du_Midi "fr:Les Journaux du Midi")] group (formerly Midi Libre SA) in 1999 and acquired a 30% stake in the "Publications de la vie catholique" in 2003, notably including *[La Vie](/wiki/La_Vie "La Vie")*, *[Courrier International](/wiki/Courrier_International "Courrier International")*, and *[Télérama](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9rama "Télérama")*, whose real estate assets were later sold.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012273-45) In 2002 and 2003, more than €60 million were raised through [bonds redeemable in shares](/wiki/Convertible_bond "Convertible bond"), increasing an already high level of long-term debt.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEveno2004679-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### 2003–2008: Crisis of the newspaper and the executive board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2003–2008: Crisis of the newspaper and the executive board")]

Main article: [La Face cachée du Monde](/wiki/La_Face_cach%C3%A9e_du_Monde "La Face cachée du Monde")

In 2003, a series of books and academic works criticized the neutrality of the newspaper and targeted the three leading figures of *Le Monde*: Jean-Marie Colombani, Edwy Plenel, and [Alain Minc](/wiki/Alain_Minc "Alain Minc"). In the journal *[Actes de la recherche en sciences sociales](/wiki/Actes_de_la_recherche_en_sciences_sociales "Actes de la recherche en sciences sociales")*, sociologist [Patrick Champagne](/w/index.php?title=Patrick_Champagne&action=edit&redlink=1 "Patrick Champagne (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patrick_Champagne "fr:Patrick Champagne")], associated with the [Bourdieusian](/wiki/Pierre_Bourdieu "Pierre Bourdieu") school, analyzed the evolution of the daily newspaper and the influence of Jean-Marie Colombani in an article entitled "Le médiateur entre deux mondes." These criticisms escalated into accusations with the publication of the essay *[La Face cachée du « Monde »](/wiki/La_Face_cach%C3%A9e_du_Monde "La Face cachée du Monde")*. In February 2003, the book by [Pierre Péan](/wiki/Pierre_P%C3%A9an "Pierre Péan") and [Philippe Cohen](/w/index.php?title=Philippe_Cohen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Philippe Cohen (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippe_Cohen "fr:Philippe Cohen")] argued, among other points, that the management team had deliberately shifted toward a logic of profitability and sales at the expense of [journalistic ethics](/wiki/Journalistic_ethics "Journalistic ethics").[[48]](#cite_note-Fottorino-2012-49)

The authors also denounced the monthly salary of the newspaper's editor-in-chief (€26,000 per month), despite an estimated group loss of €25 million for the 2003 financial year (with consolidated revenue of €460 million, the year of acquisition of the *La Vie catholique* group). According to the book, the original editorial line had been altered to serve the power objectives of a small affiliated group, involving collusion with economic elites. Alleged disregard for [raison d'État](/wiki/Raison_d%27%C3%89tat "Raison d'État") was also central to the critique. Other commentators accused the newspaper of editorial bias, claiming that it actively campaigned for [Lionel Jospin](/wiki/Lionel_Jospin "Lionel Jospin") during the [2002 French presidential election](/wiki/2002_French_presidential_election "2002 French presidential election").[[49]](#cite_note-50)

A highly publicized defamation lawsuit brought by the group[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) was ultimately resolved through mediation by [Guy Canivet](/wiki/Guy_Canivet "Guy Canivet"), First President of the [Court of Cassation](/wiki/Court_of_Cassation_(France) "Court of Cassation (France)"), in June 2004, thereby avoiding a trial.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)

This mediation was criticized by the media watchdog group [Acrimed](/w/index.php?title=Acrimed&action=edit&redlink=1 "Acrimed (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acrimed "fr:Acrimed")] as an attempt to stifle debate.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

[Alain Rollat](/w/index.php?title=Alain_Rollat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alain Rollat (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alain_Rollat "fr:Alain Rollat")], a journalist at *Le Monde* from 1977 to 2001, also sharply criticized what he viewed as managerial failings under Jean-Marie Colombani, whom he considered chiefly responsible for the growing influence of financial interests over the so-called [newspaper of record](/wiki/Newspaper_of_record "Newspaper of record"). The publication of his testimony was largely ignored by his former colleagues.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERollat2003-56)

[Daniel Schneidermann](/wiki/Daniel_Schneidermann "Daniel Schneidermann"), then an employee of *Le Monde*, criticized the newspaper's leadership in his book *Le Cauchemar médiatique*, arguing that management failed to respond substantively to the arguments made in *La Face cachée du « Monde »*. He was dismissed in October 2003 for "real and serious cause," with management claiming that a passage in his book was damaging to the company. Schneidermann sued the newspaper before the [labour courts](/wiki/Labour_court "Labour court") in Paris and won in May 2005, a decision upheld on appeal in March 2007.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Another investigative book, published the following year by [Patrick de Saint-Exupéry](/wiki/Patrick_de_Saint-Exup%C3%A9ry "Patrick de Saint-Exupéry") on the [Rwandan genocide](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide"), contributed, according to [Éric Fottorino](/wiki/%C3%89ric_Fottorino "Éric Fottorino"), to unease among journalists at *Le Monde*, as the newspaper had in 1994 endorsed what he later described as an erroneous narrative of a "double genocide" that exonerated French diplomacy.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012236-237-58)

On 29 November 2004, Edwy Plenel resigned as editor-in-chief[[58]](#cite_note-59) and left the newspaper entirely in September 2005. Colombani appointed [Patrick Jarreau](/w/index.php?title=Patrick_Jarreau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Patrick Jarreau (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patrick_Jarreau "fr:Patrick Jarreau")] as interim editor, recalling him from Washington.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012283-60)

In response to the crisis, *Le Monde* accepted a capital increase by the [Lagardère Group](/wiki/Lagard%C3%A8re_Group "Lagardère Group") and published a new format on 7 November 2005, prepared by Éric Fottorino and his think tank "Vivaldi."[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012324-326-61) According to Fottorino, the overhaul of the newspaper's structure led to a sustained rise in reader satisfaction, exceeding 80 percent.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012324-326-61)

Tensions surrounding Lagardère's role intensified. In September 2005, after 24 years of partnership with the RTL program *Le Grand Jury*, the newspaper was replaced by *[Le Figaro](/wiki/Le_Figaro "Le Figaro")*, following Lagardère's capital increase and its ownership of rival radio station [Europe 1](/wiki/Europe_1 "Europe 1").[[61]](#cite_note-62)

On 3 May 2007, Colombani publicly called for a vote for [Ségolène Royal](/wiki/S%C3%A9gol%C3%A8ne_Royal "Ségolène Royal").[[62]](#cite_note-63) Three weeks later, on 22 May 2007, the journalists' association of *Le Monde* refused to grant him a third term as head of the executive board. Subsequent leadership changes culminated in a collective resignation in December 2007, followed by Éric Fottorino's appointment as president of the executive board in January 2008.

In January 2008, the newspaper was ordered by a Barcelona court to pay €300,000 in damages for defamation in an article concerning alleged doping practices at [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona").[[63]](#cite_note-64)

The daily newspaper lost €15 million in 2007 alone, with circulation down 10 percent over four years and advertising revenue down 40 percent.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFottorino2012394-65) Facing continued debt, the group undertook restructuring measures in 2008, including the sale of several subsidiaries.

### 2009–2018: the Bergé–Pigasse–Niel trio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2009–2018: the Bergé–Pigasse–Niel trio")]

In May 2009, Éric Fottorino criticized [Nicolas Sarkozy](/wiki/Nicolas_Sarkozy "Nicolas Sarkozy") in an editorial for what he described as the president's "boastfulness and frenzy," triggering a crisis with shareholders. Billionaire [Vincent Bolloré](/wiki/Vincent_Bollor%C3%A9 "Vincent Bolloré"), a friend of the head of state, announced that he would stop printing his free daily *[Direct Matin](/wiki/CNews_(newspaper) "CNews (newspaper)")* on *Le Monde* printing presses. *[Le Journal du Dimanche](/wiki/Le_Journal_du_Dimanche "Le Journal du Dimanche")*, owned by billionaire [Arnaud Lagardère](/wiki/Arnaud_Lagard%C3%A8re "Arnaud Lagardère"), another close associate of Sarkozy, announced that it would change printers. Finally, *[Les Échos](/wiki/Les_%C3%89chos_(France) "Les Échos (France)")*, owned by billionaire [Bernard Arnault](/wiki/Bernard_Arnault "Bernard Arnault"), also a personal friend of the president, terminated its contract with the printing plant owned by *Le Monde*. According to Éric Fottorino, "power was trying to suffocate us through industrial means."

During the same period, an investigation published by *Le Monde* highlighted the central role of the bank [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas") in French crony capitalism, repeatedly citing its CEO, [Michel Pébereau](/wiki/Michel_P%C3%A9bereau "Michel Pébereau"). This episode led BNP Paribas, despite being *Le Monde'*s long-standing bank, to refuse to assist the newspaper while it was in severe financial difficulty. Fottorino later reflected that it was probably ill-timed, while negotiating the newspaper's future, to antagonize someone who held part of the solution, asking whether displeasing powerful actors condemned the paper to decline, while concluding that it was too late to turn back.[[65]](#cite_note-LeMondeDiplo_july2012-66)

In June 2010, five potential buyers were presented: *[Le Nouvel Observateur](/wiki/Le_Nouvel_Observateur "Le Nouvel Observateur")*, *[El País](/wiki/El_Pa%C3%ADs "El País")*, the Italian publishing group that produces *[L'Espresso](/wiki/L%27Espresso "L'Espresso")*, the Swiss group [Ringier](/wiki/Ringier "Ringier"), and a trio formed by [Pierre Bergé](/wiki/Pierre_Berg%C3%A9 "Pierre Bergé") (entrepreneur and owner of *[Têtu](/wiki/T%C3%AAtu "Têtu")*), [Matthieu Pigasse](/wiki/Matthieu_Pigasse "Matthieu Pigasse") (businessman, owner and president of *[Les Inrockuptibles](/wiki/Les_Inrockuptibles "Les Inrockuptibles")*), and [Xavier Niel](/wiki/Xavier_Niel "Xavier Niel") (founder of [Free](/wiki/Free_(ISP) "Free (ISP)")).[[66]](#cite_note-67)

This bid prompted a meeting between President Nicolas Sarkozy and Éric Fottorino on 9 June 2010. The head of state warned that if the Bergé–Pigasse–Niel option were chosen, the state would forgo contributing €20 million to rescue the newspaper's printing operations.[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)

At the end of June, the Bergé–Pigasse–Niel offer was overwhelmingly supported by employee shareholders. Orange and *Le Nouvel Observateur* withdrew their bids, and the choice was validated by a vote of the supervisory board on 28 June 2010 (11 votes in favor and 9 abstentions).[[69]](#cite_note-70)

On 2 November 2010, the acquisition of the newspaper by the trio was formally approved. The Le Monde Group then came under the control of the holding company *[Le Monde libre](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde_libre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Le Monde libre (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_Monde_libre_(entreprise) "fr:Le Monde libre (entreprise)")]*, which held 64 percent of the capital and was owned by the three businessmen along with the Spanish media group [Prisa](/wiki/Prisa "Prisa").[[70]](#cite_note-71)

The circumstances surrounding the sale were criticized in an article published in June 2011 by *Le Monde diplomatique*, entitled *Comment « Le Monde » fut vendu*.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

On 14 September 2010, *Le Monde* announced that it had filed a complaint against unknown persons for violation of the protection of journalists' sources, after French intelligence services were used by the executive branch to identify the source of a newsroom journalist. [Bernard Squarcini](/wiki/Bernard_Squarcini "Bernard Squarcini"), head of the [Direction centrale du renseignement intérieur](/wiki/Direction_centrale_du_renseignement_int%C3%A9rieur "Direction centrale du renseignement intérieur") (DCRI), acknowledged that he had ordered an intelligence inquiry into leaks related to the [Bettencourt affair](/wiki/Bettencourt_affair "Bettencourt affair"), an action widely viewed as infringing press freedom.

On 15 December 2010, Éric Fottorino was dismissed from his positions as chairman of the management board and publisher of *Le Monde* because of disagreements with shareholders. He was replaced as chairman by Louis Dreyfus, and on 7 February 2011, [Érik Izraelewicz](/wiki/%C3%89rik_Izraelewicz "Érik Izraelewicz") was appointed editor-in-chief of the group, a choice approved by journalists with 74 percent of the vote. Izraelewicz died suddenly on 27 November 2012, at the age of 58, after a heart attack at the newspaper's headquarters.

After an interim period led by [Alain Frachon](/wiki/Alain_Frachon "Alain Frachon"), [Natalie Nougayrède](/wiki/Natalie_Nougayr%C3%A8de "Natalie Nougayrède") was proposed on 1 March 2013 by the three main shareholders and became editor of *Le Monde* following a positive vote by the newsroom. Her mandate, alongside Louis Dreyfus, was to place the digital transformation at the center of their leadership.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

This quickly led to tensions with the newsroom. In February 2014, protests erupted following the announcement of a mobility plan that involved shifting around fifty positions toward digital operations and eliminating several sections. On 6 May 2014, seven members of the editorial management resigned, citing major dysfunctions and a lack of trust and communication with editorial leadership. Lacking support from shareholders, Natalie Nougayrède resigned on 14 May 2014, stating that she no longer had the means to fully and calmly perform her duties.

A new organizational structure was introduced on 28 May 2014. [Gilles van Kote](/w/index.php?title=Gilles_van_Kote&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gilles van Kote (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gilles_van_Kote "fr:Gilles van Kote")] was appointed interim director by the Bergé–Niel–Pigasse trio, pending a vote by the journalists' association, while [Jérôme Fenoglio](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Fenoglio "Jérôme Fenoglio") became head of the editorial staff.

On 6 October 2014, *Le Monde* launched a new layout described by its chief executive Louis Dreyfus as clearer and more open.

On 30 June 2015, following the resignation of Gilles van Kote and a second vote by the journalists' association, Jérôme Fenoglio became director of the daily newspaper, while [Luc Bronner](/w/index.php?title=Luc_Bronner&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luc Bronner (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luc_Bronner "fr:Luc Bronner")] was appointed editor-in-chief.

The fact-checking section *[Les Décodeurs](/w/index.php?title=Les_D%C3%A9codeurs&action=edit&redlink=1 "Les Décodeurs (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Les_D%C3%A9codeurs "fr:Les Décodeurs")]* was created on 10 March 2014, and on 1 February 2017, its journalists launched a search engine known as Décodex.

Following the death of [Pierre Bergé](/wiki/Pierre_Berg%C3%A9 "Pierre Bergé") in September 2017, [Xavier Niel](/wiki/Xavier_Niel "Xavier Niel") and [Matthieu Pigasse](/wiki/Matthieu_Pigasse "Matthieu Pigasse") each bought half of his shares in Le Monde libre, which then held 72.5 percent of the Le Monde Group.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

On 25 October 2018, Matthieu Pigasse sold 49 percent of his stake in the company Le Nouveau Monde to Czech billionaire [Daniel Křetínský](/wiki/Daniel_K%C5%99et%C3%ADnsk%C3%BD "Daniel Křetínský"), owner of [Czech Media Invest](/w/index.php?title=Czech_Media_Invest&action=edit&redlink=1 "Czech Media Invest (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Czech_Media_Invest "fr:Czech Media Invest")] and the weekly *[Marianne](/wiki/Marianne_(magazine) "Marianne (magazine)")*, as well as part of the magazine division of the [Lagardère Group](/wiki/Lagard%C3%A8re_Group "Lagardère Group"). This move raised concerns within the newspaper's independence pole, which described the operation as abrupt, and generated tensions with Xavier Niel.

### 2019–2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2019–2020")]

In March 2019, the [Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation](/wiki/Bill_%26_Melinda_Gates_Foundation "Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation") awarded the publishing company a grant of US$2,126,790 over three years for *Le Monde Afrique*,[[74]](#cite_note-Gates-2019-75)[[75]](#cite_note-Mediapart-2019-10-08-76) in order to support its coverage of development and [global health](/wiki/Global_health "Global health") in Africa and to inform and engage audiences through high-quality journalism.[[74]](#cite_note-Gates-2019-75)

The foundation was already a partner of *Le Monde Afrique*, having supported the project since its creation in 2015, and had previously awarded grants in this context.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)

In July of the same year, [Matthieu Pigasse](/wiki/Matthieu_Pigasse "Matthieu Pigasse") and [Daniel Křetínský](/wiki/Daniel_K%C5%99et%C3%ADnsk%C3%BD "Daniel Křetínský") negotiated the purchase of the shares held by the Spanish group [Prisa](/wiki/Prisa "Prisa"), which owned 20 percent of the Le Monde Group, triggering renewed tensions with [Xavier Niel](/wiki/Xavier_Niel "Xavier Niel"). Discussions also raised the possibility that Křetínský could take control of the holding company Le Nouveau Monde. These developments caused concern within the newsroom, which called for the preservation of editorial independence in a collective op-ed published in *Le Monde*.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

The two main shareholders, Xavier Niel and Matthieu Pigasse, agreed to sign the approval rights mechanism requested by the newsroom.

Negotiations between Le Nouveau Monde and Prisa ultimately failed, and the approval rights granted the newspaper's independence pole the power to block changes in shareholder control.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)

At the beginning of 2020, all departments of the Le Monde Group moved into a new headquarters designed by the Norwegian architectural firm [Snøhetta](/wiki/Sn%C3%B8hetta "Snøhetta"). The building is located on [Avenue Pierre-Mendès-France](/w/index.php?title=Avenue_Pierre-Mend%C3%A8s-France&action=edit&redlink=1 "Avenue Pierre-Mendès-France (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Avenue_Pierre-Mend%C3%A8s-France "fr:Avenue Pierre-Mendès-France")], in the [Paris Rive Gauche](/wiki/Paris_Rive_Gauche "Paris Rive Gauche") district of the 13th arrondissement of Paris, overlooking the railway tracks of [Gare d'Austerlitz](/wiki/Gare_d%27Austerlitz "Gare d'Austerlitz").[[82]](#cite_note-83)

In July 2020, the group announced that it had recorded a net profit of €2.6 million in 2019 on revenue of €302.7 million, driven by strong growth in its digital subscriber base.[[83]](#cite_note-84) For the third consecutive year, the group posted a positive financial result.[[84]](#cite_note-85) However, the first half of 2020, marked by the [COVID-19 pandemic in France](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_France "COVID-19 pandemic in France"), was expected to reduce group revenue by €18 million, with a 50 percent drop in advertising income.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

According to the magazine *[Marianne](/wiki/Marianne_(magazine) "Marianne (magazine)")*, the newsroom of *Le Monde*, like other left-leaning newspapers, was divided between two camps described as [multiculturalists](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") and [republican universalists](/wiki/Civic_nationalism "Civic nationalism"). The divide was said to focus less on economic and political models, such as [class struggle](/wiki/Class_struggle "Class struggle") versus [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy"), and more on social issues including [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism"), minorities, and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[86]](#cite_note-87) The emergence of the [#MeToo](/wiki/MeToo_movement "MeToo movement") movement against harassment was described as triggering a major internal crisis at the newspaper, and an article by Zineb Dryef devoted to [Assa Traoré](/wiki/Assa_Traor%C3%A9 "Assa Traoré") also prompted internal criticism. Luc Bronner, then editor-in-chief, rejected accusations that the newspaper was being overly accommodating on these issues.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

### Since 2021

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Since 2021")]

In April 2021, *Le Monde* announced the creation of an endowment fund intended to ensure the group's long-term capital independence.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/6f/67_avenue_Pierre-Mend%C3%A8s-France_Paris.jpg/250px-67_avenue_Pierre-Mend%C3%A8s-France_Paris.jpg)](/wiki/File:67_avenue_Pierre-Mend%C3%A8s-France_Paris.jpg)

The newspaper's new headquarters in 2021, located at 67–69 Avenue Pierre-Mendès-France.

On 1 January 2022, management announced a 20-cent increase in the newsstand price, citing rising production costs, particularly the price of paper.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

The daily also faced the departure of its two most prominent cartoonists. [Plantu](/wiki/Plantu "Plantu"), who had worked for the newspaper since 1 October 1972, ended his career at *Le Monde* in March 2021 after 50 years, leaving the front-page space to colleagues from the [Cartooning for Peace](/wiki/Cartooning_for_Peace "Cartooning for Peace") collective. In January 2021, the newspaper's managing editor [Caroline Monnot](/w/index.php?title=Caroline_Monnot&action=edit&redlink=1 "Caroline Monnot (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caroline_Monnot "fr:Caroline Monnot")] issued an apology for publishing a cartoon by [Xavier Gorce](/w/index.php?title=Xavier_Gorce&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xavier Gorce (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xavier_Gorce "fr:Xavier Gorce")] that could be interpreted as minimizing the seriousness of [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest") and using inappropriate language with regard to victims and [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") people.[[90]](#cite_note-91) After 18 years of collaboration, Gorce announced his departure from the newspaper, stating that "freedom is not negotiable" and criticizing pressure from social media activists.[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93)

In September 2022, the newspaper's management decided to remove from publication an opinion column by researcher Paul-Max Morin entitled "Réduire la colonisation en Algérie à une 'histoire d'amour' parachève la droitisation de Macron sur la question mémorielle" following protests from the [Élysée Palace](/wiki/%C3%89lys%C3%A9e_Palace "Élysée Palace").[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96)
According to the Élysée, the article contained a factual error resulting from a misinterpretation of remarks made by the President of France in Algiers.

In June 2024, the newspaper called on its readers to form a "republican front" against the [National Rally](/wiki/National_Rally "National Rally") in the [2024 French legislative election](/wiki/2024_French_legislative_election "2024 French legislative election").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In October 2024, the supervisory board was renewed, with Aline Sylla-Walbaum appointed as chair. A former chief of staff to [François Fillon](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Fillon "François Fillon"), she is employed by Chaumet, owned by [Bernard Arnault](/wiki/Bernard_Arnault "Bernard Arnault"), and also serves as a board member of [Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield](/wiki/Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield "Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield"), a company linked to [Xavier Niel](/wiki/Xavier_Niel "Xavier Niel"), the main shareholder of *Le Monde* and Arnault's son-in-law. She was joined on the board by Cécile Cabanis, also employed by Xavier Niel through Unibail.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

In January 2025, *Le Monde* announced its decision to leave the [X](/wiki/X_(social_network) "X (social network)") social network, citing the "intensification of activism" by [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") and the "growing toxicity of exchanges" on the platform.[[98]](#cite_note-99) The newsroom emphasized that this decision, though difficult, was part of an effort to preserve editorial independence and to avoid contributing to an environment harmful to public debate.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

## Publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Publications")]

### The daily newspaper *Le Monde*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The daily newspaper Le Monde")]

*Le Monde* is distinctive in that it is dated one day ahead of its actual publication date. In France, it is the only daily newspaper, together with *[Présent](/wiki/Pr%C3%A9sent_(newspaper) "Présent (newspaper)")*, that intended to retain this formula as of 2013. Its issue of the day is thus available from around 1:00 p.m. in Paris, [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), and [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse")[[100]](#cite_note-101) (as well as in printable digital format), and in the evening in a few major French cities, and everywhere else the following day, including internationally.

For example, an edition printed on Friday the 1st is dated Saturday the 2nd.

Although it is still commonly referred to as an "evening daily", *Le Monde* has in practice become a midday newspaper. Editorial deadlines take place in the morning at 10:30 a.m., making it possible to include news that breaks overnight or early in the morning, unlike most competing newspapers, which close their editions during the night.

As of 18 December 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit), the newspaper is structured as follows:

* **Front page**: consisting of an opinion column, very often accompanied by a current-affairs photograph; the editorial of the day in the center; a cartoon by [Plantu](/wiki/Plantu "Plantu") at the bottom of the page; and other short items developed later in the paper;
* **Page two**: notably featuring the daily press cartoon by Xavier Gorce;
* **Page three**: named for its position, this page is devoted to an in-depth investigation of a specific topic, whether linked to recent events or to longer-term investigative reporting. A large space is devoted to images;
* **Planet**: one to two pages devoted to environmental news;
* **International, International & Europe**: four to five pages devoted to international and European news;
* **France**: three to four pages devoted to French news, primarily political;
* **Economy**: two to three pages devoted to economic, financial, and industrial news;
* **Analysis**: three to four pages reserved for debates (op-eds, opinion columns, public reactions, open letters, etc.) or for in-depth analysis of a current issue;
* **Culture**: two to three pages devoted to French and international cultural news. The Wednesday issue focuses on new film releases;
* **& You**: one page devoted to practical, everyday life;
* **Obituaries**: death notices, tributes, marriages, and births;
* **Weather & games**: the weather section has no longer appeared in print since 6 October 2014 following the launch of a new formula; it is available online on the newspaper's website;
* **Final page** (generally page 28): devoted to readers' letters and an opinion column by a public figure.

Each issue of *Le Monde* includes a counter-investigation, which may concern any section of the newspaper.

While the overall structure of the newspaper usually remains almost identical from one day to the next, the editorial staff may devote additional pages to a given topic in response to major news events. For example, during its coverage of the [earthquake of March 2011 in Japan](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami"), the Planet section occupied nearly ten pages in some issues.

Since 2009, *Le Monde* has designated a "Personality of the Year". Recipients have included Brazilian president [Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva](/wiki/Luiz_In%C3%A1cio_Lula_da_Silva "Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva") in 2009 and [Julian Assange](/wiki/Julian_Assange "Julian Assange") in 2010.

For just over twenty years (2000–2021), *Le Monde* offered subscribers a PDF version of the print newspaper. In spring 2021, it informed subscribers that the PDF version was being discontinued due to fraud and unauthorized content sharing.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

### *Le Monde.fr*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Le Monde.fr")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Lemonde_fr_2005_logo.svg/250px-Lemonde_fr_2005_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lemonde_fr_2005_logo.svg)

Le Monde logo with ".fr", designed by Pierre Katz in June 2005.

*Le Monde* has been [present on the Internet](/wiki/Online_newspaper "Online newspaper") under its own [domain name](/wiki/Domain_name "Domain name") (lemonde.fr) since 19 December 1995.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Almost all of the newspaper's textual content is freely accessible on the website every day in the early afternoon. Articles less than three days old are also freely available, although without the newspaper's photographic and infographic material. Additional sources are made available to readers, including news agency dispatches and blog posts.

Access to the archives is limited for subscribers to the newspaper, who are entitled to consult up to 25 archive articles per month free of charge; beyond that limit, archive access is paid. Since April 2002, it has been possible to subscribe to the paid section of the website, providing access to agency dispatches ([AFP](/wiki/Agence_France-Presse "Agence France-Presse"), [AP](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press"), and [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters")), a database of election results updated since 1969, and multimedia content, including nearly one million *Le Monde* articles online, corresponding to the complete daily newspaper since 1987.

In addition, since September 2006, *Le Monde* has launched a new service, the *Electronic Newspaper*.[[103]](#cite_note-104) This service makes it possible to read *Le Monde* online while benefiting from features specific to digital formats, such as page-flipping, digital zoom, and search functions. In July 2008, and again in March 2012, the website's homepage was completely redesigned.

The electronic edition of the newspaper was first created in 1994. It was developed internally and distributed on electronic networks through an agreement with [CompuServe](/wiki/CompuServe "CompuServe") and Edelweb, a French company specializing in online security. The [web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") version was launched on 19 December 1995, 51 years after the first print issue, and was produced by a team of three journalists recruited by [Michel Colonna d'Istria](/w/index.php?title=Michel_Colonna_d%27Istria&action=edit&redlink=1 "Michel Colonna d'Istria (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michel_Colonna_d%27Istria "fr:Michel Colonna d'Istria")].[[104]](#cite_note-105)

Since 1999, the website has been published by the company Le Monde interactif, a majority-owned subsidiary of *Le Monde* with a 34% stake held by [Lagardère](/wiki/Lagard%C3%A8re_Group "Lagardère Group"). Le Monde interactif was initially chaired by Alain Giraudo, then by [Bruno Patino](/w/index.php?title=Bruno_Patino&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bruno Patino (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruno_Patino "fr:Bruno Patino")], following the failure of the Tout.lemonde.fr portal launched in 2000. The CEO of Le Monde interactif was subsequently [Philippe Jannet](/w/index.php?title=Philippe_Jannet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Philippe Jannet (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippe_Jannet "fr:Philippe Jannet")], who was replaced in 2012 by Isabelle André.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

### Les Décodeurs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Les Décodeurs")]

*Les Décodeurs* is a section of the *Le Monde* [website](/wiki/Website "Website"), created on 10 March 2014. According to a book by [Rémy Rieffel](/w/index.php?title=R%C3%A9my_Rieffel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rémy Rieffel (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/R%C3%A9my_Rieffel "fr:Rémy Rieffel")] and Jean-Baptiste Legavre, professors of information and communication sciences at Paris II Panthéon-Assas University and at the [Institut français de presse](/wiki/Institut_fran%C3%A7ais_de_presse "Institut français de presse"), the success of sections such as *Les Décodeurs* at *Le Monde* or "Désintox" at *Libération*[[106]](#cite_note-rieffel-107) is the result of "the valorization within newsrooms of fact-checking, intended to control the accuracy of statements made by politicians".[[106]](#cite_note-rieffel-107)

It is "a well-deserved success", according to journalist Fidel Navamuel, editor of the website "Les Outils du Web".[[107]](#cite_note-Navamuel-108) Around ten journalists work in this section, which was formally created in 2014 after operating for three years as a blog.[[108]](#cite_note-Recorbet-109)

The *Les Décodeurs* section is funded in particular by [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook").[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-Croiset2019-111)

According to Louis Dreyfus, "it is Facebook that enabled us to finance part of the growth of our teams, while respecting the integrity of the content".[[111]](#cite_note-EloïseCohen2019-112)

This partnership was strengthened in 2019, with Facebook becoming the newspaper's leading external driver of subscriptions.[[111]](#cite_note-EloïseCohen2019-112)

On 1 February 2017, three years after its creation, *Les Décodeurs* launched "Décodex", a [search engine](/wiki/Search_engine "Search engine") accessible via its website or through a [browser extension](/wiki/Browser_extension "Browser extension"). It is presented as "a tool for verifying information intended for teachers (and others)". Décodex relies on a database of hundreds of websites, mainly French but also English, American, and German. Its primary purpose is to help distinguish reliable sources from misleading, conspiratorial, or highly biased websites.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

### LeMonde.fr blogs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: LeMonde.fr blogs")]

Since the early 2000s, *lemonde.fr* allowed its subscribers to publish blogs hosted on the website. On 10 April 2019, the newspaper announced that this service would end on 5 June of the same year.[[113]](#cite_note-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115) Posts from the 400 subscriber blogs hosted by *Le Monde* were preserved by the [Bibliothèque nationale de France](/wiki/Biblioth%C3%A8que_nationale_de_France "Bibliothèque nationale de France"), with some blogs also archived by the [German National Library](/wiki/German_National_Library "German National Library").[[115]](#cite_note-116)

### Smartphone and tablet applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Smartphone and tablet applications")]

On 13 November 2008, *Le Monde* launched one of the first news applications for smartphones and tablets, initially for the [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") and [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad"). The application was available free of charge via the newly created [App Store](/wiki/App_Store "App Store").[[116]](#cite_note-117)[[117]](#cite_note-118) It was downloaded more than 100,000 times within fifteen days[[118]](#cite_note-119) and 500,000 times within six months.[[119]](#cite_note-120)

In 2011, the application "Le Monde: l'info en continu" was released on [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)").[[120]](#cite_note-121)

In 2015, a new [freemium](/wiki/Freemium "Freemium") application, *La Matinale du Monde*, became available on the [App Store](/wiki/App_Store "App Store") and [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play").[[121]](#cite_note-122)

Since 15 September 2016, *Le Monde*, along with seven other French newspaper publishers (*[Paris Match](/wiki/Paris_Match "Paris Match")*, *[Vice](/wiki/Vice_(magazine) "Vice (magazine)")*, *[L'Équipe](/wiki/L%27%C3%89quipe "L'Équipe")*, [Melty](/wiki/Melty_Group "Melty Group"), *[Cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitan_(magazine) "Cosmopolitan (magazine)")*, [Konbini](/wiki/Konbini "Konbini"), and Tastemade), has published daily exclusive content and a distinctive visual experience on Discover, the media section of the [Snapchat](/wiki/Snapchat "Snapchat") application.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

### Supplements, sections, and titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Supplements, sections, and titles")]

*Le Monde* publishes daily, weekly, and monthly supplements, as well as various occasional special supplements.[[Note 2]](#cite_note-supplements_note-124)

#### Daily

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Daily")]

* *Le Monde éco & entreprise*, a daily supplement (since May 2013), published in 8 to 12 pages. Previously a weekly supplement, it appeared with the Tuesday-dated edition of the newspaper. Titled *Le Monde économie* until April 2012, it then changed its name to the current one while keeping the same publication day and frequency (except for several weeks during summer and Christmas). This supplement contained feature stories, analyses, and interviews devoted to the economy, markets, and business life. From March 2019, *Le Monde* returned to the original format by reintegrating the Economy pages into the main section, marking the end of eight years of development of the supplement with its own front page.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

#### Weekly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Weekly")]

* *Le Monde Science & Médecine*, published as a supplement to the Wednesday-dated edition of the daily (except in August and at Christmas), comprising 8 pages. Until May 2013, the supplement was titled *Le Monde science&techno* and appeared with the "Weekend" edition dated Saturday;
* *Le Monde des livres*, founded in 1967 by [Jacqueline Piatier](/w/index.php?title=Jacqueline_Piatier&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacqueline Piatier (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacqueline_Piatier "fr:Jacqueline Piatier")],[[124]](#cite_note-126) published as a supplement to the Friday-dated edition of the daily (except in August and at Christmas), comprising 8 to 10 pages. It covers publishing news and reviews of major new releases across all genres, from classical literature to graphic novels;
* *Le Monde Idées* (formerly *Le Monde culture&idées*), published as a supplement to the Saturday-dated "Weekend" edition of the daily, comprising 8 pages. From March 2019, this supplement was discontinued and replaced by "Ideas" pages integrated into the main section;
* *[M, le magazine du Monde](/w/index.php?title=M,_le_magazine_du_Monde&action=edit&redlink=1 "M, le magazine du Monde (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/M,_le_magazine_du_Monde "fr:M, le magazine du Monde")]* (formerly *Le Monde 2* and *Le Monde magazine*), published as a supplement to the Saturday-dated edition of the daily. It features lifestyle news, fashion and beauty, design, culture, and related topics.

#### Monthly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Monthly")]

* *Le Monde Argent*, focusing on financial news (loans, savings, investments, real estate, borrowing). The supplement became *Le Monde argent & patrimoine* in November 2012, with a variable monthly publication date, then adopted its current name from February 2014;
* *Le Monde Université et Grandes Écoles*, analyzing education news for parents, teachers, and students. The supplement took the name *Le Monde universités & grandes écoles* in May 2012 and appears as a supplement to the Thursday-dated edition of the daily.

The daily newspaper also publishes more than 30 occasional special supplements each year, including *Le Monde des vins*, *Europa* (in collaboration with non-French newspapers), and supplements devoted to major cultural events (Avignon Festival, Lyon Biennale, etc.).

#### Former supplements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Former supplements")]

* *Le Monde géopolitique*, a former supplement to the Thursday-dated edition of the daily, usually comprising 8 pages. Initially titled *Le Monde géo&politique*, it was first published with the Sunday-dated edition and, from May 2013, with the Thursday-dated edition, along with a name change in September 2013. Publication of the supplement ceased with the issue dated 19 December 2013. Four geopolitics pages were reintegrated into the main section of the Sunday–Monday-dated edition of *Le Monde* from late 2015;
* *Le Monde Sports* (formerly *Le Monde sport&forme*), published as a supplement to the Saturday-dated "Weekend" edition and comprising 8 pages. Publication of this supplement permanently ceased on 18 March 2017, with two sports pages reintegrated into the main section of the Saturday-dated issue;
* *Le Monde télévisions*, published as a supplement to the Sunday-dated edition of the daily, comprising 28 or 32 pages. It covered news across all screens (television and web), as well as a selection of television and radio programs for the coming week. From 6 October 2014, this supplement was discontinued in favor of a daily page in the main section of the newspaper and three pages in the Sunday–Monday-dated edition.

#### Anthologies and selections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Anthologies and selections")]

The most significant articles published in *Le Monde* and its supplements are also collected and published in various formats:

* *Le Monde Sélection hebdomadaire* (since 1948), dated Saturday, a 12-page publication featuring the best articles from the previous week, sold exclusively by subscription;
* *[Le Monde mensuel](/wiki/Le_Monde_mensuel "Le Monde mensuel")*, a selection of the best articles from the previous month, published from February 2010 to January 2014;
* [Dossiers et Documents](/w/index.php?title=Dossiers_et_Documents&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dossiers et Documents (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dossiers_et_Documents "fr:Dossiers et Documents")], a monthly publication issued from 1973 to September 2013, aimed at high school and university students, compiling one or two dossiers per issue on economic, historical, political, or social topics.

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Governance")]

### Shareholding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Shareholding")]

*Le Monde* is a subsidiary of the [Groupe Le Monde](/wiki/Groupe_Le_Monde "Groupe Le Monde"), which also publishes *[L'Obs](/wiki/L%27Obs "L'Obs")*, *[Télérama](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9rama "Télérama")*, *[Le Monde diplomatique](/wiki/Le_Monde_diplomatique "Le Monde diplomatique")*, *[La Vie](/wiki/La_Vie_(magazine) "La Vie (magazine)")*, and *[Courrier international](/wiki/Courrier_international "Courrier international")*.

The Le Monde Group is owned:

* 75% by the holding company *Le Monde Libre*;
* 25% by the *Pôle d'Indépendance du Monde* (Le Monde Independence Trust).

*Le Monde Libre* is owned by Nouveau Monde (bringing together [Matthieu Pigasse](/wiki/Matthieu_Pigasse "Matthieu Pigasse") and [NJJ Holding](/wiki/NJJ_Holding "NJJ Holding")

([Xavier Niel](/wiki/Xavier_Niel "Xavier Niel")),[[125]](#cite_note-127) Berly Media ([Madison Cox](/wiki/Madison_Cox "Madison Cox")), and the Spanish group [PRISA](/wiki/PRISA "PRISA").

The *Pôle d'Indépendance du Monde* brings together several organizations, including the Société des rédacteurs du Monde (editors' society), the Société des lecteurs du Monde (readers' society), the Société des employés du Monde (employees' society), the Société des personnels de *Courrier international*, the staff association of *L'Obs*, and the association of minority shareholders.

### Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Independence")]

To avoid [shareholder pressure](/wiki/Editorial_independence "Editorial independence") on journalists, as can occur in some media outlets,[[126]](#cite_note-128)[[127]](#cite_note-129) *Le Monde* has adopted specific governance safeguards.

Under the newspaper's statutes, the appointment of the editor-in-chief must be approved by at least 60% of the journalists on staff.

Since 2017, following amendments to *Le Monde* statutes, the *Pôle d'Indépendance du Monde* has held a *[golden share](/wiki/Golden_share "Golden share")* protecting its statutory rights regardless of its capital share. Minority shareholders therefore have the power to block certain decisions by majority shareholders.

In 2020, Xavier Niel, one of the shareholders of the *Le Monde Libre* holding, placed all of his shares into a special endowment fund. This fund is legally inalienable. However, the researcher [Julia Cagé](/wiki/Julia_Cag%C3%A9 "Julia Cagé"), who heads the Société des lecteurs du Monde, has pointed out weaknesses in the governance of the fund, which remains under the control of Xavier Niel and his heirs.[[128]](#cite_note-130)[[129]](#cite_note-131)

The growing number of employees directly linked to Xavier Niel on the supervisory board has also raised concerns about independence, especially as some have close ties to the political sphere.[[130]](#cite_note-Canard_conseil-132)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Finances")]

Each year, the Le Monde Group shares its financial statements (balance sheet and income statement) with *Le Monde* readers.[[131]](#cite_note-133) In 2021, the group's revenue amounted to €301 million.

Subscriptions are currently the newspaper's main source of revenue.

The newsroom of *Le Monde* employs 520 journalists on permanent contracts,[[132]](#cite_note-134) not including the staffs of other Le Monde Group publications (*L'Obs*, *Télérama*, or *La Vie*).

Journalists belong to a union, the Société des rédacteurs du Monde.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

*Le Monde* also benefits from French press subsidies. From 2003 to 2010, it received €2.95 million from the press modernization fund.[[134]](#cite_note-136) In 2010, it was the second French daily newspaper receiving the most state subsidies, with €17 million in direct aid. In 2011 and 2012, it ranked first, with €16.9 million and €18.6 million respectively.[[135]](#cite_note-137)

In 2021, its subsidy amounted to €8 million.[[136]](#cite_note-138)
