## Criticism, controversies, and condemnations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Criticism, controversies, and condemnations")]

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 1970s")]

In 1976, Michel Legris published *[Le Monde tel qu'il est](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde_tel_qu%27il_est&action=edit&redlink=1 "Le Monde tel qu'il est (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_Monde_tel_qu%27il_est_(essai) "fr:Le Monde tel qu'il est (essai)")]*. According to this former *Le Monde* journalist (1956–1972), the newspaper's editorial line had shifted to the left, notably by supporting [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand"), and by adopting a conciliatory stance toward [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong")'s China.[[147]](#cite_note-149)

The [Mitrokhin Archive](/wiki/Mitrokhin_Archive "Mitrokhin Archive")—documents provided by [Vasili Mitrokhin](/wiki/Vasili_Mitrokhin "Vasili Mitrokhin"), a former [KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB") agent—also mention *Le Monde* reporting on the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), asserting that in July 1975 the newspaper used a "distorted account" of a speech by Russian dissident [Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn](/wiki/Aleksandr_Solzhenitsyn "Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn") in the United States in order to "defame him by portraying him as a Nazi sympathizer".[[148]](#cite_note-vanderMade202402-150) Although there was no evidence that the account had been introduced by a KGB agent, it was, according to Mitrokhin's book, "entirely consistent with the disinformation the KGB sought to spread in the Western press".[[148]](#cite_note-vanderMade202402-150)

In January 1977, *Le Monde* published a [petition and a statement](/wiki/French_petitions_against_age-of-consent_laws "French petitions against age-of-consent laws") written by [Gabriel Matzneff](/wiki/Gabriel_Matzneff "Gabriel Matzneff") in support of defendants in a pedophilia case (the [Versailles affair](/w/index.php?title=Versailles_affair&action=edit&redlink=1 "Versailles affair (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Affaire_de_Versailles "fr:Affaire de Versailles")]). In May of the same year, it also published an [open letter](/wiki/Open_letter "Open letter") "calling for the revision of certain legislative texts governing relations between adults and minors".[[149]](#cite_note-151)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: 1990s")]

In September 1998, Érik Izraelewicz, then editor-in-chief of *Le Monde* and previously responsible for its economic coverage, published an article in the journal *[Revue des sciences humaines](/w/index.php?title=Revue_des_sciences_humaines&action=edit&redlink=1 "Revue des sciences humaines (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Revue_des_sciences_humaines "fr:Revue des sciences humaines")]*. He explained how social news, which had previously taken precedence at *Le Monde*, was gradually merged with economic news as the latter gained prominence, and how corporate news progressively came to dominate the economic and social section.[[150]](#cite_note-152) [Serge Halimi](/wiki/Serge_Halimi "Serge Halimi"), director of *Le Monde diplomatique*, added ironically in his political essay *Le Grand Bond en arrière* (2004; reissued in 2006 and 2012): "Then an 'business' supplement is created (*Le Monde des affaires*). Finally, it will regularly be *Le Monde Argent*."

In 1999, the newspaper's editorial staff "chose intervention" in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), as acknowledged by Edwy Plenel. Journalists Pierre Rimbert and Serge Halimi accused the paper of contributing to [disinformation](/wiki/Disinformation "Disinformation") by uncritically relaying accusations made by Western governments against [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"). The newspaper devoted several front pages to [Operation Horseshoe](/wiki/Operation_Horseshoe "Operation Horseshoe") (a purported plan for ethnic cleansing by Serbia), which was in fact an invention of the German government intended to justify [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")'s entry into the war.[[151]](#cite_note-153)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In March 2021, *Le Monde* was accused by the director of the magazine *[Le Point](/wiki/Le_Point "Le Point")*, [Franz-Olivier Giesbert](/wiki/Franz-Olivier_Giesbert "Franz-Olivier Giesbert"), of having, two decades earlier under the editorship of Edwy Plenel, "conducted a disgraceful campaign" against [Dominique Baudis](/wiki/Dominique_Baudis "Dominique Baudis"), who was "falsely accused of sexual crimes before dying shortly afterward of advanced cancer". The accusation referred to an article written by one of Plenel's associates, [Jean-Paul Besset](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Besset "Jean-Paul Besset"), in which "red masses and sadomasochistic parties" were mentioned.[[152]](#cite_note-154)

In the spring of 2003, the false nature of testimonies given before the investigating judge and broadcast on television news against Dominique Baudis—who died eleven years later, in 2014—was quickly established. The emotion generated by the so-called [Alègre affair](/wiki/Patrice_Al%C3%A8gre "Patrice Alègre"), named after one of its protagonists, was later used in 2010, during the Bettencourt affair, by the government against the former editor-in-chief of *Le Monde*,[[153]](#cite_note-155) who had since become a co-founder of the investigative journalism website [Mediapart](/wiki/Mediapart "Mediapart").

On 27 September 2003, the mediator of *Le Monde* published an assessment of the newspaper's coverage of the affair, recalling that it had "avoided falling into certain traps, in particular, unlike other media outlets, the false testimony of the mythomaniac transvestite Djamel". Although "aware for a long time that Dominique Baudis's name was mentioned in police reports", the newspaper stated that it had "waited, before reporting it, until he agreed to speak and respond in our pages", and that it had consistently "reported on the counterattacks" by the former mayor of Toulouse, "to the point of being the first to reveal his denunciation of a 'political conspiracy'". The newspaper nevertheless expressed regret over "the publication of certain excerpts from investigative records, a report from the outskirts of Toulouse whose content was contradicted by the courts, and the uncorroborated account of the late testimony of a prostitute".[[154]](#cite_note-156)

The report from the outskirts of Toulouse, devoted to the search of a house by gendarmes and signed by Nicolas Fichot and Jean-Paul Besset, was dated 16 June 2003.[[155]](#cite_note-157) This was three weeks after a male prostitute questioned by gendarmes had testified on 22 May 2003, filmed from behind under the pseudonym Djamel, on TF1's 8 p.m. news, about sadomasochistic parties, claiming that there had been "deaths".[[156]](#cite_note-besset2205-158) Three days later, on 25 May 2003, he claimed on France 2's 8 p.m. news that he had seen, at these gatherings, a young girl who had disappeared in the region.[[157]](#cite_note-159)

The role of television and of the regional daily *[La Dépêche du Midi](/wiki/La_D%C3%A9p%C3%AAche_du_Midi "La Dépêche du Midi")*, which had launched a press campaign starting on 1 April 2003 on the basis of statements by two prostitutes—triggering the media frenzy that affected Dominique Baudis—was later denounced in the television film *[Notable, donc coupable](/w/index.php?title=Notable,_donc_coupable&action=edit&redlink=1 "Notable, donc coupable (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Notable,_donc_coupable "fr:Notable, donc coupable")]*, broadcast in November 2016.[[158]](#cite_note-160) The film was adapted from the book *[Le Bûcher de Toulouse](/w/index.php?title=Le_B%C3%BBcher_de_Toulouse&action=edit&redlink=1 "Le Bûcher de Toulouse (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_B%C3%BBcher_de_Toulouse "fr:Le Bûcher de Toulouse")]* by [Marie-France Etchegoin](/w/index.php?title=Marie-France_Etchegoin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marie-France Etchegoin (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marie-France_Etchegoin "fr:Marie-France Etchegoin")] and [Matthieu Aron](/w/index.php?title=Matthieu_Aron&action=edit&redlink=1 "Matthieu Aron (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matthieu_Aron "fr:Matthieu Aron")], journalists at *L'Obs* and France Info.[[159]](#cite_note-161)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: 2010s")]

In May 2011, irritated by the content of an article in *Le Monde* devoted to François Mitterrand and written by historian [François Cusset](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Cusset "François Cusset"), shareholder Pierre Bergé said that he "regretted" having invested in the daily newspaper.[[160]](#cite_note-162)

In June 2011, the monthly *Le Monde diplomatique* published an article by journalist Pierre Rimbert criticizing the gradual erosion of editorial independence at *Le Monde*. The article notably quoted a statement by billionaire and *Le Monde* shareholder [Xavier Niel](/wiki/Xavier_Niel "Xavier Niel"): "When journalists piss me off, I buy a stake in their paper and then they leave me alone".[[161]](#cite_note-163)

In July 2012, *Le Monde diplomatique* reported remarks by Éric Fottorino, former director of *Le Monde*, who stated: "Le Monde has joined the ranks of those renowned titles whose fate is now tied to capital and the goodwill of industrial or financial captains". Serge Halimi, director of *Le Monde diplomatique*, added ironically that "having been an advocate of "happy globalization," Le Monde has become its prey".[[162]](#cite_note-LeMondeDiplo_juillet2012-164)

In *Un Monde à part* (2013), Jean-Marie Colombani also criticized the newspaper's 'evolution as a result of its new shareholders, arguing that it was no longer a "journal run by journalists", but rather "engaged on the left simply by virtue of its ownership" (Pierre Bergé, Xavier Niel, Matthieu Pigasse). Because of this same ownership structure, the former editor stated that the newspaper "is no longer independent of economic power".[[163]](#cite_note-165)

Journalists at the daily have also been criticized for excessive politicization. [Adam Nossiter](/w/index.php?title=Adam_Nossiter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adam Nossiter (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adam_Nossiter "fr:Adam Nossiter")] of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* described *Le Monde* as "frenetic toward Nicolas Sarkozy and lacking distance with respect to the National Front".[[164]](#cite_note-166)

In 2013, the media watchdog association Acrimed accused *Le Monde* of participating in the near-unanimous support of French media for European austerity policies,[[165]](#cite_note-167) of failing to review certain widely successful books critical of French journalism,[[166]](#cite_note-168) and of using its brand image to sell products unrelated to journalism.[[167]](#cite_note-169)

Although the newspaper's management has denied these accusations,[[168]](#cite_note-170) the neutrality of *Le Monde'*s coverage of candidates in the French presidential election has been questioned by Acrimed and [Arrêt sur images](/wiki/Arr%C3%AAt_sur_images "Arrêt sur images"), which denounced an unacknowledged bias in favor of [Emmanuel Macron](/wiki/Emmanuel_Macron "Emmanuel Macron").[[169]](#cite_note-171)[[170]](#cite_note-172)

In October 2019, the newspaper's website mistakenly announced the death of [Bernard Tapie](/wiki/Bernard_Tapie "Bernard Tapie"). The article was quickly withdrawn.[[171]](#cite_note-173)

#### 2012 presidential election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: 2012 presidential election")]

On 10 April 2012, *Le Monde* ran a front-page headline stating that "[Marine Le Pen](/wiki/Marine_Le_Pen "Marine Le Pen") comes out on top among 18–24-year-olds", based on a survey by the [CSA Institute](/w/index.php?title=CSA_Institute&action=edit&redlink=1 "CSA Institute (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Institut_CSA "fr:Institut CSA")] conducted from 12 to 18 March 2012. In that poll, "the subsample of 18–24-year-olds included fewer than 200 people", which *Le Monde* did not disclose to its readers, according to the [Commission des sondages](/w/index.php?title=Commission_des_sondages&action=edit&redlink=1 "Commission des sondages (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commission_des_sondages "fr:Commission des sondages")].[[172]](#cite_note-174)

Other polling institutes produced different results regarding young voters' intentions. For all other polls during the [2012 French presidential election](/wiki/2012_French_presidential_election "2012 French presidential election"), *Le Monde* partnered with [Ipsos](/wiki/Ipsos "Ipsos"). Its poll dated 10 April 2012 did not yet show an increase for Marine Le Pen, who then received 15 percent of voting intentions, compared with 16 percent two weeks earlier.[[173]](#cite_note-175) Marine Le Pen ultimately obtained 18 percent of the votes among 18–24-year-olds in the first round,[[174]](#cite_note-176) almost the same proportion as in the overall electorate (17.90 percent).

## Criticism of *Les Décodeurs*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Criticism of Les Décodeurs")]

*Les Décodeurs* have prompted questions and criticism. Journalist [Vincent Glad](/w/index.php?title=Vincent_Glad&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vincent Glad (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vincent_Glad "fr:Vincent Glad")] considers the intentions laudable but highlights the difficulty of expressing an "impartial and transparent" judgment.[[175]](#cite_note-177)[[176]](#cite_note-178)

[Daniel Schneidermann](/wiki/Daniel_Schneidermann "Daniel Schneidermann") also argued in *[Libération](/wiki/Lib%C3%A9ration "Libération")* that, in this role, *Le Monde* is "judge and party." He further fears that the ranking has little effect on readers, as it would mainly be useful to those already convinced, and criticizes its tendency to favor professional media outlets, citing *[Valeurs actuelles](/wiki/Valeurs_actuelles "Valeurs actuelles")* as an example—then rated green by Décodex (later downgraded to orange).[[177]](#cite_note-179)

## 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: 2020s")]

In 2021, *Le Monde* was heavily criticized by scientists and other media outlets after publishing a summer feature perceived as overly sympathetic to [Anthroposophy](/wiki/Anthroposophy "Anthroposophy") and its [pseudoscientific](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience") tenets.[[178]](#cite_note-180)[[179]](#cite_note-181)

During the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup"), an editorial in the newspaper denounced what it described as "last-minute accusations" against host country [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar").[[180]](#cite_note-182) Critics noted that concerns about Qatar and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") had been raised since the tournament was awarded more than a decade earlier.

On 2 December 2023, the government of [Burkina Faso](/wiki/Burkina_Faso "Burkina Faso") suspended *Le Monde* from "all distribution channels" following the publication of an article on a deadly attack in the north of the country carried out by the Group for the Support of Islam and Muslims (GSIM). The newspaper was reinstated and then suspended again in April 2024, with its correspondent expelled, after publishing an article alleging massacres of civilians by the national army.[[181]](#cite_note-183)

In January 2024, controversy arose within the newsroom following the appointment of [Rayan Nezzar](/w/index.php?title=Rayan_Nezzar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rayan Nezzar (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayan_Nezzar "fr:Rayan Nezzar")], partner of political editor Ivanne Trippenbach, to the cabinet of Prime Minister [Gabriel Attal](/wiki/Gabriel_Attal "Gabriel Attal"). Several journalists raised concerns about a potential [conflict of interest](/wiki/Conflict_of_interest "Conflict of interest"), prompting the Société des rédacteurs du Monde to refer the matter to the ethics committee of the Le Monde Group. Trippenbach subsequently stepped down from her position and moved to the special correspondents' desk.[[182]](#cite_note-184)

In December 2024, an investigation by *[Le Figaro](/wiki/Le_Figaro "Le Figaro")* journalist [Eugénie Bastié](/wiki/Eug%C3%A9nie_Basti%C3%A9 "Eugénie Bastié") alleged internal unease at *Le Monde* over its coverage of Israeli policy and the Palestinian question. *Le Monde* removed internal displays referenced in the article, stating they "may have shocked" readers.[[183]](#cite_note-185)

A subsequent counter-investigation by *Off Investigation* claimed that several assertions in the *Figaro* article were false or exaggerated.[[184]](#cite_note-186) *Le Monde* also criticized the use of anonymous sources to undermine a competing outlet widely regarded by some scholars as France's newspaper of record.[[185]](#cite_note-187)[[186]](#cite_note-188)

## Judicial convictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Judicial convictions")]

In 2007, *Le Monde* was convicted of defamation against the general director of the [Grand Théâtre de Genève](/wiki/Grand_Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Gen%C3%A8ve "Grand Théâtre de Genève").[[187]](#cite_note-189)

In November 2009, *Le Monde* and its subsidiary *Le Monde Interactif* were each fined €1,500 by the Paris Criminal Court for defaming Socialist MP [Julien Dray](/wiki/Julien_Dray "Julien Dray").[[188]](#cite_note-190) The 17th chamber of the Paris Tribunal de grande instance criticized the journalist for using information from [Tracfin](/wiki/Tracfin "Tracfin") relating to an investigation into the MP, which gave it "an appearance of credibility," without warning readers of the necessary caution at that stage of the investigation; and for having "failed to exercise due care" by not giving Mr. Dray the opportunity to respond, and by not recalling the "unilateral and non-adversarial" nature of the Tracfin note. Julien Dray ultimately received only a formal warning.[[189]](#cite_note-191)

In 2012, *Le Monde* was convicted of infringing the [presumption of innocence](/wiki/Presumption_of_innocence "Presumption of innocence") of [Pierre Falcone](/wiki/Pierre_Falcone "Pierre Falcone") in relation to an article published in 2009.[[190]](#cite_note-192)

In February 2014, *Le Monde* was definitively convicted by the Spanish courts and ordered to compensate two football clubs for infringement of the right to honor. The newspaper was ordered to pay €300,000 in damages to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF") and €15,000 to [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") following an article accusing players of doping without evidence.[[191]](#cite_note-193) In rejecting *Le Monde'*s appeal in the case involving FC Barcelona, the [Supreme Court of Spain](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Spain "Supreme Court of Spain") ruled in 2011 that "the information published was not truthful, the newspaper having used inconsistent and uncorroborated data, and the journalist having insufficiently verified his sources in a case whose seriousness would have plunged the club into disrepute."[[192]](#cite_note-194)

*Le Monde* was convicted on 7 October 2016, of defamation after attributing to actor [John Malkovich](/wiki/John_Malkovich "John Malkovich") a hidden Swiss bank account in a subsidiary of [HSBC](/wiki/HSBC "HSBC").[[193]](#cite_note-195) This conviction was upheld on 24 May 2017, by the Paris Court of Appeal.[[194]](#cite_note-196) Journalists [Gérard Davet](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Davet "Gérard Davet") and [Fabrice Lhomme](/wiki/Fabrice_Lhomme "Fabrice Lhomme") were each fined €1,500, and the publication director €1,000. All three were jointly ordered to pay €10,000 in damages to John Malkovich.

On 17 December 2019, *Le Monde* and journalist Adrien Senecat were convicted by the Paris Tribunal de grande instance of public defamation against [Olivier Berruyer](/w/index.php?title=Olivier_Berruyer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Olivier Berruyer (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olivier_Berruyer "fr:Olivier Berruyer")], founder and editor of the blog *les-crises.fr*, and ordered to pay €1,500 in damages. On the same day, Samuel Laurent—then head of the *Les Décodeurs* section at *Le Monde*—was convicted for a defamatory tweet against Olivier Berruyer.[[195]](#cite_note-197)

In June 2023, *Le Monde* was convicted by the [Paris Commercial Court](/wiki/Tribunal_de_commerce_de_Paris "Tribunal de commerce de Paris") of [unfair competition](/wiki/Unfair_competition "Unfair competition") by disparagement against *[FranceSoir](/w/index.php?title=FranceSoir&action=edit&redlink=1 "FranceSoir (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/FranceSoir "fr:FranceSoir")]* and ordered to pay €25,000 in damages.[[196]](#cite_note-198) The commercial judge held that "the criticism by *Le Monde* against *France-Soir* [...] is likely to cause serious harm to its business model [...] which runs counter to free competition and freedom of trade." *Le Monde* appealed the decision, viewing it as "a serious and disproportionate infringement on freedom of criticism." An analysis by *CheckNews* of *[Libération](/wiki/Lib%C3%A9ration "Libération")* noted that the procedure was based on commercial law and was applicable only between entities in a potential competitive situation with *France-Soir* due to the "distribution of information via their websites."[[197]](#cite_note-199)

## Censorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Censorship")]

* *Le Monde* is blocked in China, including its digital edition.
* In 2009, an issue of *Le Monde* was banned from sale in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") because it contained a poll on the popularity of King [Mohammed VI](/wiki/Mohammed_VI_of_Morocco "Mohammed VI of Morocco").[[198]](#cite_note-200)
* In 2010, an issue of *Le Monde* was blocked in Morocco after publishing [WikiLeaks](/wiki/WikiLeaks "WikiLeaks") revelations deemed defamatory by the Moroccan authorities, concerning corruption in the country and implicating individuals close to the king.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

## Iconography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Le_Monde&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Iconography")]

*Le Monde au Bain Turc (after Ingres)* is a 1973 lithograph by the Peruvian painter [Herman Braun-Vega](/wiki/Herman_Braun-Vega "Herman Braun-Vega"). In this work, the artist juxtaposes the conviviality of the scene in [*The Turkish Bath*](/wiki/The_Turkish_Bath "The Turkish Bath") painted by [Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres](/wiki/Jean-Auguste-Dominique_Ingres "Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres") with the harsher reality of current events surrounding the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), as reported on the front page of *Le Monde* dated 26 January 1973.[[200]](#cite_note-202)[[201]](#cite_note-203)
